evidence: Large callback-bag runtimes aggregate many unrelated responsibilities (startup init/background orchestration, importer storage/upload/provision, and ship workflow container+urbit+snapshot+event wiring) instead of exposing narrow use-case abstractions.
Runtime API duplication preserves parallel callback names (`XFn` and non-`Fn`) and adds selector wrappers, increasing indirection without adding policy or behavior.
Multiple modules rely on mutable process-wide default runtimes (`broadcast`, `events`, docker subsystem), creating hidden coupling and bypassing explicit dependency boundaries.
The blind packet exposes only `holistic_context.structure.directory_profiles` (no `root_files` or `coupling_matrix`), reducing objective boundary validation depth for this batch.

`goseg/protocol/contracts/familycatalog/` contains 5 files and mixes protocol action-catalog concerns (`action_catalog.go`) with StarTram error-contract catalog concerns (`startram_catalog.go`) under one directory label.

README root-level governance text defines backend contract gate commands and ownership/entrypoint rules while also delegating to `docs/architecture/backend-quality-gates.md`, creating split governance documentation surfaces.

`goseg/uploadsvc/actions_test.go` duplicates contract-conformance responsibilities that are also represented in `goseg/protocol/contracts/conformance/fixtures_test.go`, indicating boundary overlap between canonical contract governance and consumer verification.

Orchestration runtime composition is spread across parallel seam structs and repeated field-by-field merge/wiring paths in goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_core.go (WithRuntimeDependencies/NewRuntimeWithDependencies/WithStartupRuntimeDependencies) and goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go (defaultRuntime* and defaultStartup* builders).

Legacy dual callback naming (e.g., Initialize and InitializeFn, LoadWireguard and LoadWireguardFn) requires normalization shims and duplicate selector methods across goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_core.go and goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go.

Global mutable default runtimes are used as operational seams in production code (goseg/docker/events/events.go, goseg/broadcast/state.go) and are repeatedly saved/restored in tests (goseg/startram/backup_runtime_test.go, goseg/startram/state_sync_test.go, goseg/importer/importer_test.go), indicating hidden shared-state coupling.

Config patch schema code combines parsing, update detection, apply semantics, merge semantics, and global registry initialization in one unit in goseg/config/config_update_schema.go, while depending on wide cross-section config shapes from goseg/structs/configs.go.

findings.summary: Runtime seam structs are oversized callback bags that mix multiple domains, reducing abstraction leverage and increasing change coupling.
evidence: Startup runtime composes many callbacks across init, host, storage, and background services (e.g., `startupInitRuntime` embedding multiple runtime groups and `startBackgroundServicesRuntime`).
Importer runtime embeds storage/upload/provision seams and wires many cross-domain callbacks in one object (`importerRuntime` + `defaultImporterRuntime`).
Workflow runtime embeds orchestration ops plus transition/event concerns in one struct (`workflowRuntime` embeds `RuntimeContainerOps`, `RuntimeUrbitOps`, and `RuntimeSnapshotOps`).
findings.suggestion: Split runtime bags into use-case interfaces per workflow step (for example: transition publisher, ship config persistence, container lifecycle, upload coordination) and inject only the minimal interface needed by each function. Keep composition in one root builder rather than passing monolithic runtimes across modules.

findings.summary: Duplicated `Fn`/legacy callback fields create dishonest interfaces where multiple names represent the same dependency.
evidence: `StartupBootstrapOps`, `StartupImageOps`, and `StartupLoadOps` each define parallel fields (`InitializeFn`/`Initialize`, `GetLatestContainerInfoFn`/`GetLatestContainerInfo`, `LoadWireguardFn`/`LoadWireguard`) and then add selector helpers to choose between them.
Downstream runtime consumers in orchestration code consistently use `Fn`-style callbacks, indicating the duplicate non-`Fn` fields are compatibility indirection rather than useful abstraction.
findings.suggestion: Complete the migration to one callback naming scheme (prefer `*Fn`), remove selector shims, and provide a temporary adapter only at boundaries that still require legacy field names.

findings.summary: Process-wide default runtimes are used as hidden service locators, weakening explicit module boundaries.
evidence: `broadcast` maintains mutable global runtime state (`defaultBroadcastStateRuntime` with getter/setter/reset).
`events` maintains mutable default broker runtime (`defaultEventRuntime` with setter/reset).
Docker subsystem and workflow paths similarly rely on package-level defaults (`defaultDockerSubsystemRuntime`, `events.DefaultEventRuntime()` in `newWorkflowRuntime`).
`main` builds behavior from default runtime constructors (`defaultBootstrapRuntime`, `defaultC2CRuntime`), reinforcing implicit dependency graphs.
findings.suggestion: Move runtime ownership to an explicit composition root and thread dependencies through constructors; keep package-level defaults only as thin compatibility wrappers that delegate to explicitly provided runtimes.

findings.summary: Broadcast lifecycle/loop APIs contain repetitive WithRuntime wrapper chains that add call depth but little behavioral value.
evidence: Lifecycle functions forward through near-identical wrappers (`bootstrapBroadcastState` -> `bootstrapBroadcastStateWithRuntime`, `LoadStartramRegionsWithRuntime` -> `LoadStartramRegionsWithRuntimeState`).

Loop APIs duplicate default/runtime variants (`StartBroadcastLoop`, `StartBroadcastLoopWithRuntime`, `runBroadcastTick`, `runBroadcastTickWithRuntime`) and include pass-through resolver (`resolveBroadcastLoopRuntime`) that only returns a field.
findings.suggestion: Keep one public default-entry function and one internal core implementation per operation; remove no-op resolver/pass-through layers and centralize nil/default handling in a single constructor.

findings.summary: Three reviewed directories are overloaded flat roots that blur ownership boundaries.
evidence: directory_profiles shows goseg/shipworkflow/ has 55 files, goseg/config/ has 41 files, and goseg/docker/orchestration/ has 34 files.
bootstrap.go contains startup/bootstrap lifecycle logic while config.go contains public config snapshots/types, but both live in the same flat config root.
container_bridge.go is runtime assembly glue and container_config.go is dispatch/router logic, but both remain in orchestration root alongside many unrelated container operations.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) create focused folders `goseg/config/bootstrap/`, `goseg/config/publicapi/`, `goseg/docker/orchestration/containerconfig/`, and `goseg/shipworkflow/newship/`; (2) move files in this order: `container_config.go` + tests, `container_bridge.go` + tests, then `newship.go`/`newship_service.go`, then `bootstrap.go`/`config.go`; (3) update package clauses/imports to preserve public entry points with thin forwarding wrappers only where needed; (4) run `gofmt -w` on moved files, then `go test ./goseg/config ./goseg/docker/orchestration ./goseg/shipworkflow`, and finally `go test ./...` to validate import/path updates.

findings.summary: New-ship boot/provisioning flow is kept in package root despite a dedicated provisioning subpackage.
evidence: newship.go is primarily a runtime wiring wrapper around `shipworkflow/provisioning` (`ProvisionShip` delegates directly to provisioning.ProvisionShip).
newship_service.go mixes transport action parsing (`HandleNewShip`) with boot orchestration entry (`HandleNewShipBoot`) and provisioning kickoff, all in the shipworkflow root.

The package already has specialized subdirectories (`shipworkflow/provisioning`, `shipworkflow/adapters/lifecyclebridge`, `shipworkflow/lifecyclewait`), indicating intended boundary decomposition that this flow bypasses.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) create `goseg/shipworkflow/newship/` with `service.go` (action parsing/reset/cancel) and `boot.go` (boot/provision entry), keeping root package for high-level orchestration only; (2) move `newship_service.go` first, then move `newship.go` runtime wiring to `newship/runtime.go`; (3) update imports and call sites to `shipworkflow/newship` entrypoints, keeping a temporary compatibility wrapper in root if needed; (4) run `gofmt -w goseg/shipworkflow/newship/*.go goseg/shipworkflow/*.go` and `go test ./goseg/shipworkflow/...`.

findings.summary: Container runtime/config assembly is split awkwardly between orchestration root and container subdomain.
evidence: container_bridge.go heavily depends on `goseg/docker/orchestration/container` types and assembles `container.LlamaRuntime`, `container.NetdataRuntime`, and `container.MinioRuntime` templates.
container_config.go acts as container-type routing/dispatch (`containerConfigBuilders` map) and is tightly coupled to bridge-produced runtime adapters.
Both files are in the orchestration root although a dedicated `goseg/docker/orchestration/container/` area already exists for container concerns.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) add `goseg/docker/orchestration/containerconfig/` for dispatch and runtime-bridge assembly; (2) move `container_bridge.go` and `container_config.go` first, then move/adjust `container_bridge_test.go` and `container_config_test.go`; (3) keep root-level façade functions (`ContainerConfigForType*`) as forwarding shims until downstream imports are migrated; (4) run `gofmt -w goseg/docker/orchestration/containerconfig/*.go goseg/docker/orchestration/*.go` and validate with `go test ./goseg/docker/orchestration/...`.

findings.summary: familycatalog directory mixes unrelated contract families, weakening ownership boundaries
evidence: `action_catalog.go` defines protocol/upload/c2c action contracts and payload rules, while `startram_catalog.go` defines StarTram error contracts with a different domain shape.
README governance entrypoints present `familycatalog/` as a single declaration hub, which reinforces the mixed-scope directory instead of family-specific ownership.
Within the same contracts area there are family-specific directories (`protocolfamily/`, `startramfamily/` in packet structure), but catalog declarations are not organized to that boundary.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) create `goseg/protocol/contracts/catalog/action/` and `goseg/protocol/contracts/catalog/startram/`; (2) move `familycatalog/action_catalog.go` into `catalog/action/` and `familycatalog/startram_catalog.go` into `catalog/startram/`; (3) keep a temporary shim package in `familycatalog/` that re-exports old symbols for one transition release; (4) update imports in contracts and tests (`go test ./protocol/contracts/... ./uploadsvc/...`); (5) remove shim and old package after callers are migrated; (6) validate full boundary health with `go test ./...`.

findings.summary: contract quality-gate policy is split between root README and architecture docs
evidence: README contains operational governance details (contract gate command, ownership notes, contract entrypoints) and separately points to `docs/architecture/backend-quality-gates.md` for detailed policy.

`uploadsvc/doc.go` and contract conformance tests encode policy semantics in-package, but root README also acts as policy text, creating multiple documentation/control planes for the same contract boundary.
This increases drift risk for compatibility policy because updates must stay synchronized across root README, architecture docs, and in-package docs/tests.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) move normative contract-governance policy to `docs/architecture/contracts-governance.md`; (2) trim README to a short pointer section only; (3) keep `uploadsvc/doc.go` to package-local behavioral contract summary and link to the single governance doc; (4) add a docs validation step in CI or release checklist (`rg -n "Contract governance entrypoints|Contract conformance gate" README.md docs/architecture/*.md` and verify one canonical source); (5) run `go test ./protocol/contracts/... ./uploadsvc/...` to confirm runtime docs/tests remain aligned after edits.

findings.summary: Runtime seam composition is duplicated across constructors and dependency-merging paths, creating drift-prone structural wiring.
evidence: goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_core.go manually merges six runtime seam groups in both WithRuntimeDependencies (lines 81-89) and NewRuntimeWithDependencies (lines 92-100), plus a parallel three-group startup merge in WithStartupRuntimeDependencies (lines 133-138).

goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go separately defines default constructors for each seam group (defaultRuntimeContainerOps/defaultRuntimeUrbit/defaultRuntimeSnapshot/defaultRuntimeStartupOps/defaultStartup*), so behavior is distributed across many hand-kept assembly points.
This pattern means adding or renaming one seam requires synchronized edits in multiple files and functions rather than one authoritative composition boundary.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a single runtime seam registry/builder API that owns merge order and default wiring for both Runtime and StartupRuntime; generate or centralize composition tables so new seams are declared once and automatically included.

findings.summary: Dual callback field conventions (legacy and Fn suffix) force normalization and duplicate dispatch logic instead of one coherent interface.
evidence: goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go defines paired callback fields in startup seams (e.g., InitializeFn + Initialize; GetLatestContainerInfoFn + GetLatestContainerInfo; LoadWireguardFn + LoadWireguard and similar pairs through line 170).

goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_core.go contains dedicated normalization shims for each paired group (normalizeStartupBootstrapOps/normalizeStartupImageOps/normalizeStartupLoadOps, lines 166-257) with repetitive assignment fallback blocks.

goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go also needs parallel callback selectors (initializeCallback/getLatestContainerInfoCallback/load*Callback methods, lines 172-233), indicating sustained compatibility complexity in the core runtime contract.
findings.suggestion: Deprecate one naming style and migrate to a single callback field per operation; provide a short-lived adapter layer at construction boundaries only, then remove normalize/callback-selector duplication.

findings.summary: Process-wide mutable runtime singletons act as implicit dependencies, increasing cross-test and cross-module coupling.
evidence: goseg/docker/events/events.go exposes mutable global runtime state via DefaultEventRuntime/SetDefaultEventRuntime/ResetDefaultEventRuntime (lines 33-55).
goseg/broadcast/state.go follows the same global mutable runtime pattern with DefaultBroadcastStateRuntime/SetDefaultBroadcastStateRuntime/ResetDefaultBroadcastStateRuntime (lines 166-186).

Assigned tests repeatedly snapshot and restore package globals (e.g., defaultAPIClient/defaultConfigService/defaultRetrieveStateSyncer in goseg/startram/state_sync_test.go lines 59-66 and 103-110; defaultAPIClient/defaultBackupInfrastructureService in goseg/startram/backup_runtime_test.go lines 94-97 and 129-132), and importer tests consume default event runtime channels directly (goseg/importer/importer_test.go lines 32-54).
findings.suggestion: Move runtime dependencies behind explicit constructors/context objects and pass brokers/stores into services and handlers; keep global defaults only as thin bootstrap wrappers, not mutable state used by core logic and tests.

findings.summary: Config patch schema file combines too many responsibilities (field schema, parsing, applying, merging, registry lifecycle), obscuring extension boundaries.
evidence: goseg/config/config_update_schema.go defines patch data model, generic field meta-type, parsing callbacks, apply callbacks, and merge callbacks in one file (e.g., configPatchField and newConfigPatchField at lines 105-270).

The same file owns global registry lifecycle and validation (configPatchByKey/init/buildConfigPatchByKey/validateConfigPatchFields at lines 303-334) and request parsing entrypoint (buildConfigPatch at lines 417-431).

Patch behavior depends on many domains from goseg/structs/configs.go (connectivity/runtime/startram/auth/penpai), while large test coverage in goseg/config/config_test.go indicates broad behavior surface concentrated into one implementation unit.
findings.suggestion: Split into explicit modules: field catalog declaration, parser/validator, applier, and merge strategy; keep a small coordinator that wires them. This preserves generic helpers while reducing responsibility mixing and simplifying future section additions.
